hero.headline: Report scams, search patterns, <highlight>stay ahead</highlight> of fraud
hero.subtitle: AVASC helps victims compare cases, follow verified scam profiles, and receive critical alerts—without exposing sensitive details in public.
hero.alertsNote: Realtime alerts and daily highlights are in the panel on the right. <link>Subscribe for SMS & email</link> in the section below.
hero.alertsPanelSubtitle: Critical warnings as they surface, plus staff-reviewed daily highlights. Indicators are public-safe; follow links for full profiles.

mission.missionV: Victim-centered intelligence and recovery guidance—not a law firm or agency.
mission.databaseV: Anonymized indicators and published scam profiles you can search safely.
mission.alertsV: Realtime SMS for critical threats plus optional daily or weekly email digests.
mission.trustV: We never ask for passwords, full card numbers, or government IDs in public forms.

footer.tagline: Victim-centered reporting, pattern intelligence, and recovery resources.
footer.disclaimerBody: Not a law firm, investigator, or government agency. No guarantee of fund recovery. Public entries are anonymized—never share passwords, full card numbers, or IDs in public forms. Watch for recovery scams with upfront fees.
footer.pending501c3Title: IRS 501(c)(3) application is pending. Donations are not currently tax-deductible. See the donate page FAQ for details.

about.metaDescription: Association of Victims Against Cyber-Scams (AVASC): mission, privacy-first design, and anti-scam support.
about.intro: AVASC is a nonprofit anti-scam platform built to support victims with dignity. We combine structured incident reporting, privacy-safe pattern intelligence, and practical recovery guidance.
about.do1: Help victims submit secure, structured scam reports.
about.do2: Analyze scam indicators and cluster recurring patterns.
about.do3: Publish anonymized public scam profiles for prevention.
about.do4: Issue <alerts>public scam alerts</alerts> and a weekly "This Week in Scams" briefing.
about.do5: Offer free <guides>scam-prevention education</guides> — guides, resources, and an AI Scam Check.
about.do6: Provide support-request workflows and recovery checklists.
about.not4: Not a place to publish private victim information.
about.privacyBody: Reports are private by default. Public database pages use anonymized, victim-safe summaries only. We do not expose private narratives, internal admin notes, or other victims' identifiable information in public views.

blog.metaDescription: AVASC's daily scam-awareness posts: a rotating look at scam spotlights, red flags, statistics, survivor stories, and where to get help.
blog.title: AVASC Blog
blog.intro: A daily scam-awareness post, rotating through spotlights, red flags, statistics, survivor stories, and where to get help — drawn from AVASC's own data and guidance.
blog.emptyBody: The first daily post is on its way. In the meantime, explore <briefings>Scam News</briefings> or our <guides>prevention guides</guides>.

briefings.metaTitle: This Week in Scams — Weekly Fraud Briefings
briefings.metaDescription: AVASC's public-safe weekly scam intelligence: what our database is seeing plus this week's authoritative fraud warnings and enforcement, with protective guidance for the public.
briefings.title: Weekly Fraud Briefings
briefings.intro: A plain-English, victim-centered read on this week's scams — combining what the AVASC database is seeing with authoritative warnings and enforcement from the FTC, FBI IC3, CISA, FinCEN, and state Attorneys General. Every briefing links out to its sources.
briefings.errorBody: Please try again in a few minutes. In the meantime, explore our <guides>scam prevention guides</guides> or the <database>scam database</database>.
briefings.emptyBody: The first "This Week in Scams" briefing is on the way. Until then, browse our <guides>prevention guides</guides>.
briefings.indexDisclaimer: This is public-safe intelligence: briefings surface only indicators and patterns that are already public, and never operational detail that would help a fraudster. AVASC is not a law firm, investigator, or government agency.
briefings.detailDisclaimer: This briefing is public-safe intelligence: it surfaces only indicators and patterns that are already public, alongside protective guidance — never a playbook for scammers. AVASC is not a law firm, investigator, or government agency, and this is not legal or financial advice. If you've been targeted, you can <report>report a scam</report> or explore <recovery>recovery resources</recovery>.

alerts.defaultSubtitle: Live scam intelligence with realtime warnings and daily analysis.
alerts.allQuiet: All quiet — no current critical alerts.
alerts.realtimeExplain: REALTIME alerts fire only for high-confidence CRITICAL scam activity. <sub>Subscribe</sub> to get them the moment they publish.
alerts.dailyNotPosted: Daily briefing not yet posted for today.
alerts.dailyExplain: AVASC publishes daily scam-pattern briefings on weekdays. <sub>Subscribe by email</sub> to get each day's brief in your inbox, or <report>report a scam</report> to contribute to the next one.

stories.metaDescription: Read real stories from scam survivors and share your experience to help others.
stories.pageIntro: Comments and posts are moderated. External links are blocked in comments for safety.
stories.emptyTitle: No published stories yet — yours could be the first.
stories.emptyBody: AVASC reviews every submission before publication to protect contributors and keep the record accurate. Use the form on the right to share what happened to you — anonymously if you prefer. If you'd rather report the scam directly instead, <report>file a case</report>.
stories.shareNote: Stories are moderated before publication. Sign in required.
stories.moderationNotice: Story content is moderated for safety and privacy. Personal contact details and financial identifiers are not allowed.
stories.reportSimilarDesc: Share your experience and help us identify scam patterns.
stories.searchDbDesc: Look up reported scam indicators and patterns.
stories.recoveryDesc: Get guidance on recovering from a scam.
stories.whatToDoTitle: What to Do If You've Been Scammed
stories.detailMetaDescription: Moderated survivor story from the AVASC community.

database.patternBlurb: This page summarizes a repeat pattern from anonymized reports. It does not identify a specific person and is not legal advice.
database.noIndicators: No public indicators are available for this profile yet.
database.profileDisclaimer: Public profiles are based on anonymized reported patterns and moderated cluster data. AVASC does not guarantee that every fact pattern is identical, and AVASC is not a law firm.
database.searchDescription: Explore staff-reviewed profiles using public-safe indicators—domains, wallets, emails, aliases, platforms, and scam types. Your search stays private.
database.resultsFound: {count, plural, one {# scam profile found} other {# scam profiles found}}
database.realtimeAlertsDesc: Subscribe to critical SMS alerts and daily or weekly scam intelligence by email.
database.tip1: Search by domain, wallet, phone, email, alias, or platform.
database.tip2: Use filters to narrow by scam type, risk, or indicator type.
database.tip3: If a profile looks familiar, report a matching case.
database.publicSafetyDesc: Results include only published scam profiles and publicly approved indicators. AVASC does not publish private victim information.
database.emptyBody: Try a broader keyword such as a domain, wallet, alias, platform, or scam type. If this looks similar to your case, consider reporting it so the pattern can be reviewed.
database.searchPlaceholder: Search by domain, wallet, phone, email, alias, platform, or scam type
database.unavailableBody: We're having trouble loading the scam pattern database right now. Please try again in a few minutes. In the meantime:
database.entityIntro: Aggregated indicator — not an accusation against any one person. Pattern data only.
database.detailsRestricted: Details restricted — this case is not published with a public summary.
database.entityMetaTitleWith: Is {value} a scam? — reported pattern profile
database.entityMetaDescription: Aggregated, public-safe scam-pattern intelligence for this indicator. Not an accusation against any individual — reported-pattern data only.

donate.metaDescription: Support AVASC's mission to help scam victims, expand scam awareness, and build tools that prevent future fraud.
donate.heroTitle: Support Scam Victims. Help Stop the Next Scam.
donate.heroSubtitle: Your donation helps AVASC support victims, build scam intelligence tools, and expand public awareness.
donate.customAmountBody: Choose one-time or monthly, then continue to our secure payment provider.
donate.impactBody: Every contribution helps AVASC build better tools, support victims, and turn reported scam experiences into public protection.
donate.impact50: Helps review and organize scam reports
donate.impact250: Helps support outreach and scam awareness efforts
donate.whyBody: Your support helps us do three things well: assist victims, organize scam intelligence, and prevent future harm.
donate.why1Body: Help people facing fraud access guidance, resources, and practical next steps.
donate.why2Body: Strengthen a structured database that helps people compare scam patterns and recognize warning signs.
donate.why3Body: Turn reported experiences into education, awareness, and protection for others.
donate.chooseTitle: Choose how you want to give
donate.chooseBody: Monthly support helps AVASC grow more steadily, while one-time gifts help fund immediate work.
donate.optMonthlyBody: Provide steady support that helps AVASC plan, build, and assist more victims over time.
donate.optOneTimeBody: Make a direct contribution to support platform development and victim resources.
donate.optPaypalBody: Use PayPal if that is your preferred way to give.
donate.missionBody: AVASC exists so people facing scams have somewhere calm, practical, and trustworthy to turn. We focus on tools and guidance that reduce isolation, organize evidence, and help prevent repeat harm.
donate.faqBody: A few quick answers before you donate.
donate.faq1A: Donations support AVASC’s platform development, victim support resources, scam awareness, and outreach.
donate.faq2A: Yes. Donations are processed through trusted payment providers such as Stripe and PayPal.
donate.faq3A: AVASC is a California nonprofit currently applying for IRS 501(c)(3) tax-exempt status. Until the IRS issues a determination letter, donations are not tax-deductible for federal income-tax purposes. We will update this page the moment our 501(c)(3) status is confirmed. If tax deductibility is important to you, please wait to contribute, or email give@avasc.org for the current status.
donate.faq4A: No. AVASC is not a law firm and does not guarantee recovery.
donate.faq5A: Yes. Monthly giving is one of the most helpful ways to support AVASC’s mission.
donate.finalCtaTitle: Help us turn pain into protection.
donate.finalCtaSubtitle: Too many scam victims face fraud alone. Your support helps build a better response.
donate.disclaimer: Donations support AVASC’s platform development, scam awareness, victim support resources, and outreach. AVASC is not a law firm and does not guarantee recovery.

guidesIndex.metaDescription: Learn how to identify scams, protect yourself, and recover with AVASC's comprehensive educational guides on romance scams, cryptocurrency fraud, investment schemes, and more.
guidesIndex.intro: Knowledge is your best defense against fraud. Explore our comprehensive guides to learn how to identify common scams, protect yourself, and recover if you've been victimized. Each guide provides actionable insights based on real scam patterns.
guidesIndex.questionsBody: If you think you've been scammed or need additional support, our team is here to help.
guidesIndex.aiIntro: Not sure which guide fits? Tell us what happened in your own words and we'll give you tailored, practical next steps. This is general guidance, not legal or financial advice.
guidesIndex.aiPlaceholder: e.g. Someone I met online convinced me to invest in a crypto app. I sent $3,000 and now they want more before I can withdraw…
guidesIndex.aiSafetyNote: Do not include passwords, full card numbers, your SSN, or 2FA codes.
guidesIndex.aiError: Something went wrong. Please try again in a moment.
guidesIndex.guide_identify_title: How to Identify a Scam: Warning Signs Everyone Should Know
guidesIndex.guide_identify_desc: Learn the most common red flags and pressure tactics scammers use to manipulate victims.
guidesIndex.guide_recovery_title: What to Do If You've Been Scammed: Step-by-Step Recovery Guide
guidesIndex.guide_recovery_desc: Immediate actions to take, reporting procedures, and resources for emotional support.
guidesIndex.guide_romance_title: Romance Scam Warning Signs: How to Protect Yourself Online
guidesIndex.guide_romance_desc: Recognize catfishing tactics, love bombing, and financial manipulation in online relationships.
guidesIndex.guide_crypto_title: Common Cryptocurrency Scam Types and How to Avoid Them
guidesIndex.guide_crypto_desc: Understand pig butchering, fake exchanges, and cryptocurrency investment fraud.
guidesIndex.guide_investment_title: Investment Scam Red Flags: How to Spot Fraudulent Schemes
guidesIndex.guide_investment_desc: Learn to identify Ponzi schemes, unregistered investments, and pressure-based tactics.
guidesIndex.guide_phishing_title: How to Protect Yourself from Phishing Emails and Fake Websites
guidesIndex.guide_phishing_desc: Spot fake emails, verify senders, and secure your accounts against digital fraud.
guidesIndex.guide_elder_title: Elder Fraud Prevention: Protecting Seniors from Scams
guidesIndex.guide_elder_desc: Understand scams targeting seniors and safeguards for protecting older adults.
guidesIndex.guide_social_title: Social Media Scams: How to Stay Safe on Facebook, Instagram, and TikTok
guidesIndex.guide_social_desc: Recognize fake giveaways, impersonation accounts, and marketplace fraud.
guidesIndex.guide_job_title: Job Scam Warning Signs: How to Spot Fake Employment Offers
guidesIndex.guide_job_desc: Learn to recognize fake job postings, work-from-home scams, and upfront fee requests.
guidesIndex.guide_tech_title: Tech Support Scam Protection: Don't Fall for Fake Computer Warnings
guidesIndex.guide_tech_desc: Identify fake virus popups, cold calls claiming to be Microsoft or Apple, and remote access tricks.
guidesIndex.guide_shopping_title: Online Shopping Scam Prevention: How to Shop Safely Online
guidesIndex.guide_shopping_desc: Learn to identify fake websites, counterfeit products, and unsafe payment methods.
guidesIndex.guide_identity_title: Identity Theft Protection: How to Safeguard Your Personal Information
guidesIndex.guide_identity_desc: Protect your SSN, monitor credit, freeze accounts, and detect data breaches early.
guidesIndex.guide_mule_title: Money Mule Scams: How Criminals Use Innocent People to Move Money
guidesIndex.guide_mule_desc: Learn how people get recruited, the legal consequences, and what to do if involved.
guidesIndex.guide_charity_title: How to Verify a Charity and Avoid Donation Scams
guidesIndex.guide_charity_desc: Verify 501(c)(3) status, check Charity Navigator, and avoid fake charities after disasters.
guidesIndex.guide_bec_title: Business Email Compromise (BEC): How Scammers Target Companies
guidesIndex.guide_bec_desc: Learn about CEO fraud, invoice manipulation, vendor impersonation, and prevention policies.
guidesIndex.guide_sextortion_title: Sextortion and Online Blackmail: What to Do and How to Get Help
guidesIndex.guide_sextortion_desc: Learn what sextortion is, why you shouldn't pay, how to report, and where to find support.

recovery.metaDescription: Find guidance and resources for recovering from cyber-scams.
recovery.title: Recovery center
recovery.intro: Grounded first steps and warnings — not legal advice. Pair this with a structured <report>scam report</report> so our team can triage and match patterns safely.
recovery.cryptoPoint1: Stop sending funds; rotate credentials if a platform was compromised.
recovery.cryptoPoint2: Export transaction hashes, wallet addresses, and chat logs (redact personal IDs).
recovery.cryptoPoint3: Report to your exchange and local cybercrime unit where available.
recovery.bankPoint1: Call your bank’s fraud line immediately with dates and amounts.
recovery.bankPoint2: Request recall / SWIFT investigation for wires when timing allows.
recovery.bankPoint3: Preserve emails, SMS, and call logs that show misrepresentation.
recovery.romancePoint1: Cease contact with the scammer; do not accept new “helpers” from the same thread.
recovery.romancePoint2: Document platform handles and payment trails.
recovery.romancePoint3: Seek emotional support from trusted people or counselors — shame is a tactic.
recovery.fakePoint1: Anyone promising guaranteed return for an upfront fee is highly suspect.
recovery.fakePoint2: Legitimate agencies do not ask for gift cards or crypto to “unlock” funds.
recovery.fakePoint3: Report the new scam attempt to AVASC with prior case reference if you have one.
recovery.evidence2: Indicators: domains, numbers, wallets, transaction IDs
recovery.evidence3: Screenshots and PDFs (upload after account creation where required)
recovery.resourcesBody: Filing with the relevant U.S. agency creates an official record, helps investigators spot patterns, and is often required by banks or insurers. Filing with AVASC does not replace these — we encourage you to do both.
recovery.ftcBody: reportfraud.ftc.gov — the U.S. Federal Trade Commission’s consumer fraud intake for scams, imposters, and deceptive business practices.
recovery.ic3Body: ic3.gov — the FBI’s Internet Crime Complaint Center. File here for cyber-enabled fraud, wire fraud, crypto theft, and cross-border schemes.
recovery.identityBody: identitytheft.gov — FTC-run recovery planner that generates a personalized step-by-step plan if your identity or accounts were compromised.
recovery.storiesBody: Learn from others who've been scammed and recovered.
recovery.reportBody: Submit a detailed report to help identify scam patterns.
recovery.databaseBody: Look up indicators and check scam profiles.

report.metaDescription: Submit a scam report confidentially. Your report helps protect others.
report.headerDescription: Structured intake helps staff link your experience to known patterns and protect others. Sensitive details are never published without your consent.
report.matchedBody: Staff can use this context when reviewing your case. <link>View pattern</link>
report.phHeadline: e.g. Fake exchange blocked my withdrawal
report.phDescription: Timeline, what they promised, and what went wrong.
report.phEvidence: Domains, wallet addresses, phone numbers, app names…
report.allowFollowUp: Allow AVASC to follow up if we need clarification (recommended).
report.disclaimer: By submitting, you confirm the information is true to the best of your knowledge. AVASC is not a law firm or government agency.
report.doneThanks: Your report helps others avoid scams. Thank you.
report.doneTrackNote: Signed-in reporters can track status from the dashboard.
report.errTitle: Add a short headline (at least 3 characters).
report.errDescription: Please add a bit more detail (at least 20 characters).
report.errContactMethod: How were you contacted? (e.g. WhatsApp, email).
report.errEvidence: Add indicators or notes (at least 10 characters).
report.errSubmitFailed: We couldn’t submit your report. Please try again.
report.errGeneric: Something went wrong. Please try again.

resources.metaTitle: Scam Resources — Report, Learn & Get Help
resources.metaDescription: A curated directory of trusted places to report a scam, learn how to protect yourself, check before you trust, and find victim support — from government agencies and established nonprofits.
resources.ogTitle: Scam Resources — Report, Learn & Get Help | AVASC
resources.ogDescription: Trusted places to report scams, learn online safety, verify before you trust, and get victim support.
resources.heading: Scam resources
resources.intro: Trusted places to report a scam, learn how to protect yourself, check before you trust, and find support if you've been targeted. We link only to government agencies and established nonprofits. Reporting to <report>AVASC</report> helps us spot patterns, but it doesn't replace filing with these official channels.
resources.group1Blurb: Filing an official report creates a record, helps investigators spot patterns, and is often required by banks or insurers. Reporting to AVASC does not replace these — do both where you can.
resources.ftcReportFraud: The U.S. Federal Trade Commission's intake for scams, imposters, and deceptive practices.
resources.fbiIc3: The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center — cyber-enabled fraud, wire fraud, and crypto theft.
resources.identityTheftGov: FTC-run recovery planner that builds a personalized step-by-step plan if your identity was compromised.
resources.cfpb: Report problems with banks, lenders, and other financial companies to the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau.
resources.sec: Securities and Exchange Commission tips, complaints, and referrals for investment schemes.
resources.fcc: Report robocalls and smishing (scam texts) to the Federal Communications Commission.
resources.group2Blurb: Free, authoritative education to help you and the people you care about recognize scams before they cause harm.
resources.ftcConsumerAdvice: Plain-language articles on how the latest scams work and how to avoid them.
resources.aarpFraudWatch: Scam alerts, prevention tips, and a free helpline — helpful for older adults and their families.
resources.cisa: The U.S. cybersecurity agency's simple steps to stay safe online.
resources.ncsa: StaySafeOnline — practical online-safety guidance for individuals and small businesses.
resources.googleScamReady: Interactive tips to spot and avoid common online scams.
resources.bbbScamTips: Better Business Bureau guidance on avoiding the most-reported scams.
resources.group3Blurb: Look something up before you send money, click a link, or accept an offer.
resources.bbbScamTracker: Search and report scams by type and location to see what others are experiencing.
resources.ftcScamAlerts: Timely warnings about scams that are active right now.
resources.chainabuse: Community reports of malicious crypto wallet addresses and scam platforms.
resources.group4Blurb: If you have been targeted, you are not alone — and it is not your fault. These services offer real help.
resources.cybercrimeSupport: FightCybercrime.org — guidance and support for individuals and businesses recovering from cybercrime.
resources.idtheftCenter: Free, confidential help for victims of identity theft and related fraud.
resources.aarpHelpline: Talk it through with a trained specialist (877-908-3360) — you don't have to be an AARP member.
resources.lifeline988: If the stress feels overwhelming, call or text 988 (US) for free, confidential support, 24/7.
resources.ncmec: Report sextortion or online exploitation involving a minor to the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children.
resources.disclaimer: External links are provided for convenience and education. AVASC is not affiliated with these organizations and is not responsible for their content. Always reach a service by typing its address yourself rather than following links from an unexpected message.
